does: This paper synthesizes security research on LLM agents through a comprehensive framework covering 247 papers. It models agent security around three core principles: information flow, delegated authority, and persistent state. The work identifies and categorizes threat surfaces and attack families specific to agentic systems where LLMs plan, invoke tools, maintain memory, and act on external environments. It systematically documents proposed defenses across different protection layers while analyzing their tradeoffs. The paper provides structured answers to fundamental questions about modeling LLM agent security, understanding which threats are most critical, evaluating available defenses, and assessing security claims through rigorous evaluation methodologies.
connects: This work builds on prior research in LLM safety and robustness that initially focused on text generation risks like jailbreaks and toxic outputs. It extends this foundation by addressing the qualitatively different threat landscape created when LLMs transition from conversational interfaces to software agents with real-world capabilities. The paper synthesizes insights from adversarial machine learning, software security, and AI safety communities while responding to the growing fragmentation of agent security research across disparate attack families, defense mechanisms, and evaluation standards. It combines systems-thinking approaches from traditional cybersecurity with emerging concerns about LLM decision-making failures in agentic contexts.
enables: This synthesis enables more rigorous and comprehensive agent security research by providing a unifying framework for understanding threat surfaces, attack vectors, and defenses. It establishes common vocabulary and evaluation standards that allow researchers to build cumulatively rather than in isolation across different application domains. The lifecycle-based perspective enables development of more holistic security architectures that coordinate defenses across information flow, privilege delegation, and state management. The systematic evaluation taxonomy supports creation of benchmarks and testing methodologies for LLM agent security. This work points toward integrated security frameworks that treat agentic systems as complex sociotechnical systems requiring coordinated defenses at multiple layers rather than isolated safety patches.
